text.title: Go concurrency: primitive selection, loop-var capture, and agent gotchas
text.summary: Decision guide for choosing a Go concurrency primitive, plus the most common LLM concurrency mistakes and how to prevent them.

rule.statement: Classify the workload before picking a primitive: IO-bound batch with "all or none" → errgroup + semaphore. Long-lived queue consumer → worker pool. Stages with transform → pipeline. Single-flight de-dup → singleflight. Rate-limited outbound → token-bucket or semaphore.
rule.rationale: Using a worker pool for "all or none" work forces custom error aggregation that errgroup provides for free. Using errgroup for a long-lived consumer requires restarting on every error.

rule.statement: For IO-bound work, set concurrency to a tunable limit (10–100x CPU count depending on remote service tolerances), not runtime.NumCPU(). Document the source of the chosen value.
rule.rationale: NumCPU() is appropriate for CPU-bound parallel work. IO goroutines spend most time waiting; NumCPU() severely under-utilizes the available parallelism.

antipattern.description: Loop variable capture (pre-Go 1.22): for _, x := range xs { go func() { use(x) }() }
antipattern.reason: All goroutines capture the same loop variable and read its final value. Force x := x before the go statement, or use Go 1.22+ range semantics.

antipattern.description: Adding a default: branch to a select that waits on a channel and ctx.Done().
antipattern.reason: default: converts the blocking select into a busy-loop, consuming 100% CPU while waiting.

antipattern.description: Forgetting close(jobs) after all work is submitted in a worker pool.
antipattern.reason: Workers wait on the channel forever. Tests hang until go test -timeout kills them. Always defer close(jobs) after the submit loop.

antipattern.description: Mixing sync.Mutex and channels on the same shared state.
antipattern.reason: Two concurrency models on one variable creates a category of bugs that neither model's tools can catch. Pick one: mutex for shared state, channels for message passing.
